entry.title: SAP Certified Associate - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Financial Accounting C_TS4FI perm. deni
entry.content:
Hi together,

I got the SAP Learning Student Edition and while I can enroll in expert live sessions, practice systems and implementation consultant certification exams for various topics, whenever I try to visit the webpage for the SAP Certified Associate - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, Financial Accounting (C_TS4FI) certification I get the following page:

The strange thing is, I get this message on different accounts, regardless of the subscription I have on the respective account. Also, why would I be able to enroll in courses meant for this certification, when I'm not allowed to take the exam.

I'm assuming it isn't really a permission error, rather SAP hasn't finalized the respective certification exam yet, or am I wrong?

Thanks for your help,

Fabian

entry.title: Re: Impact of Not Settling a Maintenance Order in SAP S/4...
entry.content:
Dear @JK_,

TECO is not a financial close and the order can still get costs. SAP explicitly states that after Technical Completion the system reduces open PRs/reservations/capacities but the order can continue to receive costs for example given invoice receipts for delivered/used materials it’s blocked for changes, not blocked for all follow-on cost postings

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-asset-management/explaining-operational-overhead-maintenance-4wm-

So TECO without settlement is normal but it leaves the order as an open cost collector

If you don’t settle the order keeps an open balance. SAP describes settlement as the step that transfers costs from the maintenance order to receivers and the balance on the order becomes zero

If you skip settlement:

  • Debit balance remains on the maintenance order
  • Costs are not transferred to the intended receiver commonly the reference object cost center but also WBS or fixed asset

More details below:

https://learning.sap.com/courses/implementing-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition-asset-management/explaining-period-end-closing-maintenance-orders-bf7-_ffb678d5-4ed4-49fe-aac1-2fd5ac6fc4c5

SAP is very clear that the settlement profile of the order type governs whether full settlement is required before business completion/closing

Option A: If your maintenance order types are processed according to phases

Default behavior:

  • Actual costs must be settled in full
  • The order must be fully settled for all relevant ledgers before business completion and closing
  • Otherwise you get error message when you try to close the order

Option B: If the order type is not processed according to phases

SAP business completion can be performed even if not fully settled but:

  • costs may remain on the order and
  • you cannot settle further costs after it has been completed for business

This is a key risk if you business complete/close too early and then late invoices arrive, you can end up with costs you can’t settle unless you reverse the status / correct the situation

Financial reporting vs controlling reporting:

  • The expense postings still exist in FI/Universal Journal settlement is mainly about moving them to the correct receiver (cost center/WBS/asset) for management accounting and downstream processes
  • Controlling / cost center reporting will be wrong or incomplete until settlement is done because the costs remain parked on the order instead of the final receiver
  • If the receiver is a fixed asset delaying settlement can delay correct capitalization-related postings

Open balances at closing:

SAP guidance frames period-end closing for maintenance orders precisely as settling to receivers for financial reporting and zeroing the order balance, so leaving orders unsettled at month/year-end typically means:

  • open balances remain on maintenance orders
  • extra reconciliation effort
  • and potentially reopening periods later if you need settlement postings and the period is already closed process/control risk

Best practice in Public Cloud:

SAP recommended operating model is:

  • maintenance orders are usually settled automatically at regular intervals via a background job or manually if not set up
  • Use the appropriate settlement tooling SAP provides Run Settlement Actual to settle actual costs from an order to receivers

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c56f622a2edf491b9f1b596b55587009/65511a912abd45518fdab7d995304e47.html

Practical safe rule:

1. TECO when work is done expect late invoices

2. Keep settling periodically and do a final settlement at month-end before you attempt business completion

3. Only CLSD once you’re confident no more relevant costs will arrive or your setup still allows settlement after business completion but SAP warns you may not be able to

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Mapping custom retention field into the supplier invoice
entry.content:
Hello @sans16

Yes, there is. You can print retention on the first output but only if you read it in the PO Output BAdI when you run output.

Here is what I suggest you do:

  • Use MMPUR_PO_OUTPU_HEADER_EXT and MMPUR_PO_OUTPUT_ITEM_EXT
  • While implementing the BAdI, read the standard PO retention field and pass it to the form context used by your custom form template.
  • Ensure output timing is set so the form is generated after output processing, not from an old snapshot item and trigger output and save.

Make sure you read standard retention directly during output enhancement, then the first output can contain it. Do not rely on a separate custom CDS populated after saving it for this scenario.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Triggering SAP Datasphere/BDC Task Chains from SAP BTP ABAP Cloud using the Task Chain REST API
entry.content:
Our modern data landscapes thrive on automation. In this post, we’ll walk end-to-end through a real-life integration scenario that tackles one of the building blocks in this context:

Starting a SAP Datasphere Task Chain remotely from SAP BTP ABAP Cloud using OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials and the Datasphere Task Chain REST API

The goal is to orchestrate Datasphere data processing flows directly from ABAP Cloud — securely, programmatically, and fully automated.

We will cover the below points:

  • Creating a Task Chain in Datasphere
  • Configuring OAuth clients in Datasphere
  • Setting up Communication System & Arrangement in ABAP Cloud
  • Executing the Task Chain remotely: Consume REST API from ABAP Cloud

1 Architecture Overview

Solution Architecture

ABAP Cloud Task Chain Execution: ABAP Cloud acts as the caller. Datasphere exposes the Task Chain API protected by OAuth.

2 Prerequisites

You must have:

  • SAP Datasphere tenant with developer + admin access
  • A Task Chain to trigger
  • SAP BTP ABAP Environment (ABAP Cloud) with developer access
  • BTP ABAP: Authorization to configure OAuth clients and Communication Arrangements

3 Creating a Task Chain in SAP Datasphere

The first step is defining a Task Chain that encapsulates the processing logic you want to execute remotely.

In the given example:

  • The Task Chain starts with a Begin node
  • Executes a Replication Flow for loading dimension data
  • (Can be extended with transformations, views, or further flows)

SAP Datasphere Task Chain with Replication Flow inside

Main takeaways:

  • Task Chain must be Deployed
  • Note the Technical Name (used later by API)
  • Verify it runs manually before you automate via the API 

4 Configuring OAuth Client in SAP Datasphere

To allow ABAP Cloud to call Datasphere APIs securely, we create an OAuth Client in Datasphere Administration.

Navigation Path

Datasphere → Administration → App Integration → OAuth Clients

OAuth Endpoints Provided

Amongst others, SAP Datasphere automatically exposes:

  • Authorization URL
  • Token URL

These are required for OAuth2 token retrieval. Moreover you must create a technical user that has access to the Datasphere space and is able to run the task chains that are in scope. Typically this is done by assigning a scoped role.

Configured OAuth Client (based on Technical User)

Menu Path Datasphere

OAuth Client Generation

OAuth Client Details

Important settings:

Parameter

Value

Grant Type

Client Credentials

Purpose

Technical User

Roles

space access must be given (e.g. by scope role in DSP)

Token Lifetime

e.g. 60 minutes

Save the Client ID and Client Secret — this is used in ABAP Cloud later on.

 5 Creating a Communication System in ABAP Cloud

Now we configure the outbound technical connection from ABAP Cloud to Datasphere.

Navigation: ABAP Environment Web Access → Communication Management → Communication Systems

ABAP Cloud Web Access

ABAP Cloud Communication Systems

The hostname is your Datasphere tenant URL. Outbound OAuth URLs (like Token/Authentication) can be found on the Datasphere side under Administration -> App Integration (previous step).

ABAP Cloud Communication Systems Details

ABAP Cloud Communication Systems Users

Key fields:

Field

Example

System ID

DSP_REST

Host Name

<your-datasphere-host> (tenant URL)

Port

443

OAuth 2.0 Endpoints

From Datasphere for Token & Authorization URL (Audience URL not required)

Client ID

Datasphere OAuth Client ID

Client Secret

Datasphere Secret (you must take note of this when creating the OAuth Client)

6 Creating a Communication Arrangement in SAP ABAP Cloud

Now we bind the Communication System to a Communication Scenario that allows REST calls.

Navigation: Communication Management → Communication Arrangements

Arrangement Example: ZDSP_CS_REST

ABAP Cloud Communication Arrangements

Note that the service path. The dynamic parts of the path are set from ABAP.

ABAP Cloud Communication Arrangements Detailed Configuration

What this provides:

  • Preconfigured service URL
  • OAuth token handling by ABAP runtime
  • Secure outbound API consumption

7 Calling the Datasphere Task Chain API from ABAP Cloud

Now comes the fun part: consuming the REST API. Datasphere exposes endpoints such as:

  • POST /api/v1/datasphere/tasks/chains/<space_id>/run/<task_chain_technical_name>
  • GET /api/v1/datasphere/tasks/logs/<space_id>/<log_id>

Which triggers a new execution or reads the status of a task chain run.

ABAP Cloud Implementation Pattern

In ABAP Cloud when working with REST API to integrate external services, you typically:

  1. Create HTTP destination automatically from the created Communication Arrangement
  2. Use CL_WEB_HTTP_CLIENT_MANAGER
  3. Perform POST/GET requests
  4. Parse response (run ID, status)

The code snippet performs the following parts in more detail:

  1. Run as a class-run program (IF_OO_ADT_CLASSRUN) -> you can execute directly from ADT and print to the console.
  2. Locate the configured Communication Arrangement (bound to our Communication System DSP_REST) using CL_COM_ARRANGEMENT_FACTORY.
  3. Create an HTTP destination from the Communication Arrangement using CL_HTTP_DESTINATION_PROVIDER=>CREATE_BY_COMM_ARRANGEMENT.
  4. Create an HTTP client from the destination (CL_WEB_HTTP_CLIENT_MANAGER).
  5. POST request to the Task Chain “run” endpoint:
    • triggers a new run
    • receives a JSON response containing a LogId
  6. WAIT a few seconds.
  7. GET the log endpoint using the returned LogId:
    • retrieves the execution status (RUNNING, later SUCCESS / FAILED, etc.)
  8. Print everything in the ABAP Console.

ABAP-Code Snippet:

CLASS zcl_dsp_rest_api DEFINITION PUBLIC FINAL CREATE PUBLIC.
   PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun.

    CLASS-DATA:
      out TYPE REF TO if_oo_adt_classrun_out.
    CLASS-METHODS:
      call_dsp
        RAISING
          cx_http_dest_provider_error
          cx_web_http_client_error.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_dsp_rest_api IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
    zcl_dsp_rest_api=>out = out.

    TRY.
        call_dsp( ).
      CATCH cx_web_http_client_error
            cx_http_dest_provider_error INTO DATA(exception).
        out->write( exception->get_text( ) ).
    ENDTRY.

  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD call_dsp.

    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_log,
             logId  TYPE i,
           END OF ty_log.

    DATA: ls_log TYPE ty_log.
    DATA(communication_system) = 'DSP_REST'.
    DATA(arrangement_factory) = cl_com_arrangement_factory=>create_instance( ).

    DATA(comm_arrangement_range) = VALUE if_com_arrangement_factory=>ty_query-cs_id_range(
      ( sign = 'I' option = 'EQ' low = communication_system ) ).

    arrangement_factory->query_ca(
      EXPORTING
        is_query = VALUE #( cs_id_range = comm_arrangement_range )
      IMPORTING
        et_com_arrangement = DATA(arrangements) ).

    DATA(arrangement) = arrangements[ 1 ].

    DATA(destination) = cl_http_destination_provider=>create_by_comm_arrangement(
      comm_scenario  = 'ZDSP_CS_REST'
      service_id     = 'ZDSP_REST_SRV_REST'
      comm_system_id = arrangement->get_comm_system_id( ) ).

    DATA(http_client) = cl_web_http_client_manager=>create_by_http_destination( destination ).
    DATA(request) = http_client->get_http_request( ).
    request->set_uri_path( '/chains/BDCPOC/run/TC_DIM2_A001' ).

    DATA(response) = http_client->execute( if_web_http_client=>post ).

    CALL METHOD /ui2/cl_json=>deserialize
      EXPORTING
        json = response->get_text( )
      CHANGING
        data = ls_log.

    data(log_str) = CONV string( ls_log-logId ).
    out->write( 'Task Chain started. LogId created:' && log_str ).

    WAIT UP TO 10 SECONDS.

    CONCATENATE '/logs/BDCPOC/' log_str INTO DATA(uri).

    clear: request, response, http_client.
    http_client = cl_web_http_client_manager=>create_by_http_destination( destination ).
    request = http_client->get_http_request( ).
    request->set_uri_path( uri ).
    response = http_client->execute( if_web_http_client=>get ).
    out->write( 'Task Chain Log REST API - via GET - Task Chain Status:' && response->get_text( ) ).

  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

Once triggered:

  • Datasphere creates a new Task Chain run
  • Execution can be monitored in Datasphere UI
  • API returns run ID and status

Follow-up calls can fetch:

  • Execution status
  • Logs
  • Completion result

8 Execution Results

ABAP Cloud Console Log: Results

The Task Chain in Datasphere is in Running status:

SAP Datasphere Task Chain Running Status

9 Conclusion/Outlook & Further aspects:

Overview of the major building blocks:

Capability

Result

Secure Auth

OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials

No hardcoded secrets

via Communication Arrangements/System

Remote orchestration

Creating an end-to-end orchestrated chain of tasks

Cloud-native ABAP

Using appropriate ABAP Class APIs

Datasphere automation

Using Task Chain API

Use Cases that the implementation enables:

  • Nightly batch orchestration
  • Event-driven data loads
  • Cross-system workflows (this was our ultimate focus)
  • CI/CD data pipelines
  • API-driven analytical data refresh

References

  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain API Documentation: https://api.sap.com/api/DatasphereTasks/overview
  • SAP Datasphere Task Chain Help Page: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_DATASPHERE/c8a54ee704e94e15926551293243fd1d/274f2736465c4c48a091c675880502a2.html?locale=en-US
  • ABAP Cloud - how to call external APIs: https://jacekw.dev/blog/2022/oauth-client-credentials-from-abap-cloud/

These are your main takeaways to further sharpen the solution:

  • Use short token lifetimes
  • Separate technical OAuth users
  • Monitor task runs via API from ABAP
  • Handle retries & failures in ABAP
  • Log run IDs for observability

If you have any aspects, comments and/or concerns, please raise them and let's discuss. I'm happy and proud that this article was written by at least 90% human and only 10% AI 🙂

entry.title: Unwanted message in app Review Customer Project
entry.content:
In app Review Customer Project after the following steps the system gives unwanted message "You are leaving the page. Any entries you have made in the form will be lost."

  1. Change ETC
  2. Recalculate - get message "EAC and PoC were recalculated."
  3. Set Forecast Status to Completed and Save - get message "Forecast status of project BREN28012026 updated."
  4. Click Back




------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Unwanted message in app Review Customer Project
entry.content:
To avoid the message "You are leaving the page. Any entries you have made in the form will be lost." the user must follow the steps below to ensure the ETC / Work Package change is carried out:

  1. Change ETC
  2. Recalculate - get message "EAC and PoC were recalculated." - this is an optional step as step 3 will also Recalculate
  3. Save - get message "Work package changes were saved."
  4. Set Forecast Status to Completed and Save - get message "Forecast status of project BREN28012026 updated."
  5. Click Back

entry.title: Release for Billing of Billing Request Lines of an Ad Hoc Billing Plan on Service Order Item
entry.content:
In the blog Billing Plan Template in Service Order Process I have explained how you can create ad hoc billing plans on service order item with the usage of billing plan templates.

In this blog, I am going to explain how you can release billing request lines of such ad hoc billing plans for billing. As for other items, the billing document requests (BDRs) that are generated will appear in the billing due list.

First, you need to check if the service order items that have an ad hoc billing plan are Released or Completed. In addition, the entire service order must be Released at least. If this prerequisite is met, you have two options to create billing document requests (BDRs). Creation of BDRs is referred to as release for billing.

  • You can use the Release for Billing app in Fiori Launchpad or in standalone WebUI.
  • You can use the report Generate BDRs for Service Orders that you can find on the SAP Easy Access menu. Just to add, you can manually schedule jobs by using this report.

Generate Billing Document Requests using a Report

You open the SAP Easy Access menu Service -> Service Processes

You click on Generate BDRs for Service Orders. (See screenshot in Figure 1.) 

Figure 1: SAP Access Menu for Generate BDRs for Service Orders (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

The report CRMS4_SERVORD_BDR_GENERATE is executed and its selection screen is opened. (See Screenshot in Figure 2.)

Figure 2: Selection Screen of Report CRMS4_SERVORD_BDR_GENERATE (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

You can find the upper frame with title Service Order/Confirmation Details. You can use these selection criteria to precisely define which billing request lines should be included.

I also would like to share the great information that you can use this report also for billable service order and billable service confirmation items. I will explain which settings are required for that also in the following section.

  • BDR Creation Horizon (in Days): The BDR Creation Horizon defines how many days ahead the system should look when creating BDRs. When you run the report, it only processes billing request lines with billing dates that fall between today and your specified number of days in the future. The system automatically calculates this date range and creates BDRs for all eligible billing request lines within that timeframe.
  • Billing Date: You can define the desired billing date range for processing.
  • Service Order ID: You can specify the range of service order ID to process. This range will be considered only if you also select the Billable Service Order Items checkbox
  • Service Confirmation ID: You can specify the range of service confirmation IDs to process. This range will be considered only if you also select the Billable Confirmation Items checkbox.
  • Sold-To Party: You can specify the range of sold-to parties.
  • Sales Organization: You can specify a sales organization.
  • Distribution Channel: You can specify the range of distribution channels.
  • Division: You can specify the range of divisions.
  • Items with Billing Plans: This checkbox is selected by default. This indicates that billing request lines are included.
  • Billable Service Order Items: You can select this checkbox to include billable service order items.
  • Billable Confirmation Items: You can select this checkbox to include billable service confirmation items.

If you want to create BDRs for billing dates in the past, you can use the selection criterion Billing Date instead of BDR Creation horizon (in Days).

In the selection screen you can find the lower frame with title Processing Details. (See Screenshot in Figure 2.)

  • Simulation Only: You can select this checkbox if you want to do a test run.
  • Parallel Processing: You can select this checkbox to improve the job performance.
  • Max. Number of Parallel Tasks: You can maintain the maximum number of parallel tasks between two and five
  • Max. Number of Billable Items: You can maintain the maximum number of billable order and confirmation items for which you want to create BDRs.

For example, you want to know how many BDRs can be created for BDR creation horizon 10 days. You enter 10 as horizon, select the checkboxes for Items with Billing Plans, Billable Service Order Items, Billable Confirmation Items, and select the Simulation Only checkbox. You click on Execute button and the Display logs screen is opened. (See Screenshot in Figure 3.)

Figure 3: Display logs screen in simulation mode (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

For example, you want to create BDRs immediately for certain service confirmations. You enter the IDs as range. You click on Execute button and check the outcome. (See Screenshot in Figure 4.)

Figure 4: Display logs screen (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

Generate Billing Document Requests using a WebUI app

You can release billable service order, and confirmation items for billing in the Release for Billing app. (See Screenshot in Figure 5.)

Figure 5: Release for Billing app (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

Starting with 2025 FPS1, billing request lines can also be released for billing in the Release for Billing app.

For this purpose, the filter criteria are enhanced. You click on Adapt Filters button. (See Screenshot in Figure 6.)

Figure 6: Adapt Filters button (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

In the newly opened window Adapt Filters, you click on the drop-down list of the first filter so that the whole list is displayed. You can check that two new filters are added: BDR Creation Horizon (in Days) and Billing Date. (See Screenshot in Figure 7.) These parameters correspond to the selection fields of the report CRMS4_SERVORD_BDR_GENERATE.

Figure 7: Adapt Filters window (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

In the search result list, you can check that two new columns are added, Ad Hoc Billing and Billing Date. (See Screenshot in Figure 8.)

Figure 8: Adapt Filters window (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

The column Ad Hoc Billing is enabled as checkbox and indicates if this line belongs to an ad hoc billing plan.  You can use the billing date to identify the billing request line of the ad hoc billing plan of service order item.

In the example that you can see in the screenshot in figure 9, the Ad Hoc Billing checkbox for item number 10 is selected for two billing dates. That means that two billing request lines of the ad hoc billing plan on service order item 10 are found.

The third Ad Hoc Billing checkbox is not selected, so item 20 is a billable service order item.

Figure 9: Adapt Filters window (screenshot from SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025).

Conclusion

Thank you for taking the time to read this blog! Hopefully you will have more in-depth knowledge about the possibilities to release items for billing in service order process.

Please don’t hesitate to raise your questions in Q&A forum of SAP Community or leave your comments below!

For more details, please visit the full product assistance documentation in the SAP Help Portal: Billing of Service Transactions | SAP Help Portal

entry.title: SAP Service and Asset Manager Phase model Work Orders
entry.content:
Hi experts!

 

I would like to know if anyone implemented the phase model work orders in SSAM to clear some of my doubts. 

1. Is it possible to create phase based work orders in SSAM?

2. Is it possible to create phase based work orders in SSAM with Auto release configuration? Because, i imagine that if i try to create a phase based work order in SSAM released, it will give me a error message related to approval of work order.

3. Is it possible to configure for one type of work order to auto release and orders not in SSAM? 

 

Best Regards,

Rafael EIj Umeda

SAP Service and Asset Manager

entry.title: SAP Community Voice: SAP BW News, Ask Anything to SAP Champions, and Events from Tokyo to Brazil!
entry.content:
For this week's edition of the SAP Community Voice, we will walk through exciting featured blog posts that have been trending over the last weeks in Technology Blogs, plus a review of the top new January features for SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere.  Also, are you looking for answers from experts? Have you always dreamed of connecting with our SAP Champions? Then this is your moment, because the Ask Me Anything initiative with SAP Champions launched this month! 

And we know that many of you who are watching right now also participated in Devtoberfest last year. Guess what? The winners have been announced! And we will tell you more details today! 

We have upcoming events taking place all over the world, including Brazil, Germany, and Tokyo, giving our community multiple opportunities to connect, learn, and engage globally.

Today's edition is super exciting and filled with news that we know you would love to know more about. Let’s dive in! 

Featured Blog Posts

Basics Information you should know about Custom Filters in Fiori elements 

SAP Champion Mio Yasutake wrote an informative blog post of the basics of creating the Custom Filters in Fiori elements. Starting with how custom filters are used in Fiori elements when standard filter fields are not sufficient, for example when a different control is required or when entered values must be transformed before being converted into OData filter conditions. Mio gradually explains how custom filters work, including the role of the internal Filter Values model, and walks through the different implementation approaches depending on whether custom logic is required. With clear explanations, code examples, diagrams, and references to a working GitHub repository, her post provides a solid foundation for anyone looking to understand and correctly implement custom filters in Fiori elements. 

Read Mio Yasutake Blog Post here 

Top January Features of SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere News  

2026 started with many great new features in SAP Business Data Cloud and Datasphere, and SAP´s Klaus Peter Sauer made sure to collect the most important ones to our SAP Community in his latest YouTube Video (click here for the video) and in a blog post. From Task Chains and Scheduling to Replication Flow, the January top features contains many technical enhancements but also one of the major milestones for BW modernization scenarios: Metadata extraction of SAP BW 7.5 is now enabled for the catalog plus also a data lineage from SAC story down to the BW 7.5 objects. And it does not stop there, because at the end of his post you will find the dates and links to sign up for the Webinar Series: The Next Era of Business Data. Make sure to take a look and save in your calendar, because this can be an opportunity to have an overview of SAP Business Data Cloud and guide you through its integral pillars and strategic vision. 

Read Klaus Peter Sauer Blog Post here 

Guide on SAP PO to SAP CI Migration Simplified File-Based Scenarios 

SAP Champion Punith Oswal wrote in his latest blog post about one of the most usual challenges during SAP PO to SAP Cloud Integration (CI) migration, which is handling file-based interfaces that rely on internal on-premise file systems. When teams move to SAP Integration Suite, many realize that SAP CI does not provide a native NFS adapter. Starting from the question, “How do we migrate scenarios where files are stored inside the customer’s internal network?,” Punith shares his solution by going through each necessary section step by step, with very useful images to make it easier to follow. If migrating file-based scenarios from SAP PO to SAP CI has turned into a roadblock for you, this content is must-read. 

Read Punith Oswal Blog Post here 

Less Code More Value: Declarative Programming in CAP 

SAP´s Brigit Selbach wrote a blog post, introducing the declarative approach in the SAP Cloud Application Programming Model (CAP) to reduce repetitive validation handlers and complex imperative code by describing what should happen instead of how to implement it. Brigit walks through the core concepts of declarative programming in CAP, including declarative expressions, input validations using built-in annotations, expressions as annotation values, and the new @assert feature released in December 2025. With clear examples covering queries, calculated elements, validations, and authorization scenarios, the blog shows how declarative programming leads to less boilerplate code, consistent validations, improved readability, and better maintainability across both CAP Java and CAP Node.js applications. 

Read Brigit Selbach Blog Post here 

Community News 

Ask Me Anything Sessions with SAP Champions 

SAP Champion and SAP BTP architect Sebastiano Marchesini introduced a new monthly AMA (Ask Me Anything) initiative launched by the SAP Champions, starting this month. Based on experience at SAP TechEd, learning sessions, and live events, the idea is getting direct honest answers from experts, which creates more connection and high trust relationships with other SAP community members. Each month, a different SAP Champion with a specific area of expertise will answer both technical and non-technical questions, submitted openly or anonymously. For this first edition, selected questions will be answered in a recorded video published on the SAP Communities YouTube channel. This AMA session will be open for questions until the last week of February. Check Sebastiano latest blog post here, for more details on how to participate. This initiative helps us to shape an open space for real discussions, practical experience, and shared learning within the SAP ecosystem. 

Hobbies Series: Growing Together Through Dog Agility 

The Hobbies Series have been one of the greatest exchange of experiences inside out Community Corner Blog Posts, and last, and last week we were impacted by the inspiring and adorable relationship between SAP Champion Patrizia Rossi and her dog, Joker, and their journey with Dog Agility Sport (read the full blog post here). What started as simple walks in the park evolved into a demanding and rewarding sport that requires discipline, focus, problem-solving skills, and a deep connection between handler and dog. In this sport, Joker and Patrizia are what is called a team (or handler–dog pair), and they train together every Friday, which has also turned out to be a wonderful way to end the working week and completely switch off. Patrizia compares this a bit to machine learning: step by step, the dog learns the mechanisms, and over time it takes little for him to understand what to do. In the end, her hobby is not just about competition, but about finding real success in stepping off the field tired but fulfilled, knowing they both gave their best, learned something new, and strengthened their connection once again. 

Devtoberfest 2025: Winners Announcement! 

The wait is finally over! The Devtoberfest 2025 winners have been announced, and we want to thank our SAP community for the incredible participation and engagement throughout the event. Congratulations to all the winners who will be joining us at SAP TechEd this year, and a big shout-out to all finalists for their dedication. Do you want to know if you are one of the winners?! Check out the full list of their names in the blog post here! 

Events 

SAP CodeJam Roadshow 2026 - Brazil edition 

SAP CodeJam Roadshow 2026 is back in Latin America, with a special edition in Brazil. The tour kicks off on March 1, with stops in several states across the country. Our experts will travel across Brazil to deliver CodeJams on Generative AI, SAP Joule, and much more. In addition, during the same weeks, we will also have SAP Inside Tracks, organized by amazing members of the local communities. And, of course, to make sure there are plenty of opportunities to talk and connect, we will also have meetups! 

Check out our agenda below: 

  • March 2 - Campinas, Brazil 
    Build AI Services using CAP and Generative AI Hub: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/build-ai-services-using-the-cap-and-generative-ai-hub-campinas-brazil/ev-p/14317691 
  • March 3 - São Paulo, Brazil 
    Joule Studio: Create Joule Skills and Agents Without Coding: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/joule-studio-create-joule-skills-and-agents-without-coding-s%C3%A3o-paulo-brazil/ev-p/14317678 + SAP Meetup – São Paulo: https://community.sap.com/t5/s%C3%A3o-paulo-events/meet-up-s%C3%A3o-paulo-2026-03-03-brazilroadshow2026/ec-p/14319322#M38 
  • March 4 - São Paulo, Brazil 
    SAP Build: Create Event-Based Processes: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/sap-build-create-event-based-processes-s%C3%A3o-paulo-brazil/ev-p/14317705 
  • March 5 - Rio de Janeiro, Brazil 
    Getting Started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-codejam/getting-started-with-generative-ai-hub-on-sap-ai-core-rio-de-janeiro-brazil/ev-p/14317716 
  • March 13 - Joinville, Brazil 
    Getting Started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core 
  • March 14 - Joinville, Brazil 
    SAP Inside Track – Joinville: SAP Inside Track Joinville 2026 - SAP Community 
  • March 20 - Belo Horizonte, Brazil 
    Getting Started with Generative AI Hub on SAP AI Core 
  • March 21 - Belo Horizonte, Brazil 
    SAP Inside Track – Belo Horizonte 

And it doesn’t stop there: the roadshow continues in April as well! Keep an eye on posts here and on the official SAP Community announcements page, as we still have more cities to announce, with events scheduled between April 6 and 17. You will not want to miss this opportunity, right? So, register using the official links, as seats are limited! For more details, check the full blog post here. 

SAP Inside Track Tokyo 2026  

SAP Inside Track Tokyo 2026 is coming soon, bringing the local SAP community together for two hybrid events focused on sharing knowledge and real-world experiences. The event will take place on February 18 and March 6, 2026, from 6:00 PM to 10:00 PM (JST), and will be held in Japanese. Participation is free of charge, and registrations can be completed via the SAP Community event page. This year’s agenda will cover Business Data Cloud and Business AI, with the official theme to be announced soon. Whether you join on-site in Tokyo or online, SAP Inside Track Tokyo offers a fantastic opportunity to connect, learn, and engage. 

Code Connect 2026: Call for Proposals Open! 

We are excited to announce the return of Code Connect for its third edition, bringing together UI5con, reCAP, and HANA Tech Con in one unique event taking place from July 13 to 16, 2026, in St. Leon-Rot, Germany, and online. While attendee registration opens on April 10, 2026, the Call for Proposals is already open and runs from January 26 to March 13, 2026, inviting speakers to actively shape the agenda across all three events. (Links to submit your application: Call for proposals UI5con, Call for proposals reCAP and Call for proposals HANA Tech Con). Code Connect offers a unique opportunity to experience three specialized events in one location: UI5con, reCAP, and HANA Tech Con, allowing you to dive deep into various aspects of SAP development. Code Connect is designed for developers at every level: Whether you are an SAP veteran or just starting out. For more details and FAQ, please make sure to check the latest blog post here.

entry.title: How can an inbound REST GET/POST request from a backend system be conditionally routed in SAP PI/PO
entry.content:
How can an inbound REST GET/POST request from a backend system be conditionally routed in SAP PI/PO based on the presence of an HTTP header parameter (ucasurl), such that:

  • If the HTTP header parameter ucasurl is present, the message is routed to a receiver REST channel that:

    • Uses a dynamic target URL derived from the value of ucasurl, and

    • Executes an HTTP GET call; and

  • If the HTTP header parameter ucasurl is not present, the message is routed to an alternative receiver REST channel that:

    • Uses a static, preconfigured target URL, and

    • Executes an HTTP POST call?

The exchange of electronic documents or report submission failed while executing the IFLOW_HTTP_COMMAND action because of an unexpected error in the process orchestration

{"source":"/default/sap.s4.gco.edoc/gpeclnt500","processInstances":[{"id":"000D3ADDB9461FE180CC540FE3BAE37E","processType":"CUSTOMER_INVOICE","processSubtype":"B2B","processVersion":"1.0.0","countryRegion":"PL","status":"RECEIVED","followupRequired":"","followupActions":"","documents":[],"messages":[]}]}

entry.title: Re: Copy feature from one address type to another
entry.content:
Hi @Proaims2512 @mohdsansari ,

There is no option to copy data from one address type to another within the address portlet. An old influence request existed for this, but it has been closed. Please raise a new request.

For Emergency Contacts, however, the system does allow copying the employee’s address within the Emergency Contact portlet - https://me.sap.com/notes/0002517885 
Regards,
VR

entry.title: AI in Management Accounting – We Need Your Input!
entry.content:
Dear SAP Community,

Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping management accounting. However, there is often a gap between technological vision and the operational reality of a management accountant’s daily work. To ensure AI is not just a buzzword but a tool that delivers value, we need to understand: Where do the actual challenges in management accounting lie?

SAP investigates together with the Technical University of Munich (TUM) how AI applications can be specifically designed to solve existing challenges in management accounting. We want to identify concrete, value-generating use cases. 

To achieve this, we rely on your expertise. Let’s tackle the problems where support is needed most!

  • Target group: Experienced professionals working within the field of management accounting.
  • Duration: Approx. 5-7 minutes.
  • Privacy: The survey is completely anonymous.

We invite you to be part of this study and help shape the next generation of SAP solutions.

 Click here to take the survey: https://preview.userzoom.com/mpaap/MTAgQzg4M1M5MzMx/1

 

Thank you for your support and insights!

entry.title: Clean Core in 2026: Can We Truly Achieve It Without a BTP‑First Mindset?
entry.content:
Clean Core is now a mandatory principle for S/4HANA transformations, but many organizations still treat SAP BTP as an optional add‑on rather than the primary extension layer.

In your experience:

  • Is Clean Core achievable without shifting custom logic, governance, and integration to BTP?

  • What are the biggest blockers you’ve seen?

  • Are customers still customizing the core because of regulatory pressure, timelines, or lack of BTP skills?

Would love to hear real‑world examples—successes or failures.

entry.title: SAP Joule + BTP + S/4HANA: What Does the Next‑Generation Intelligent Core Look Like?
entry.content:
SAP Joule is being positioned as the AI assistant across SAP’s portfolio. Combined with BTP’s AI services and S/4HANA’s Clean Core architecture, we’re entering a new era of “intelligent operations.”

I’m curious how the community sees this evolving:

  • Where will Joule add the most value in S/4HANA and Utilities?

  • Will Joule replace custom dashboards, Fiori apps, or even some workflow logic?

  • How will AI‑driven insights change governance, operations, and user experience?

  • What skills will architects need in this new landscape?

Looking forward to hearing your perspectives on how Joule will reshape SAP architecture.

entry.title: What Is the Most Overlooked Clean Core Risk in S/4HANA Projects?
entry.content:
We talk a lot about custom code, extensibility, and BTP side‑by‑side development. But in real projects, the biggest Clean Core risks often come from unexpected places:

  • Variant governance

  • Background job misconfigurations

  • Over‑authorization

  • Hard‑coded integrations

  • Shadow spreadsheets

  • Uncontrolled Z‑tables

  • Manual corrections in production

In your experience, what is the most overlooked Clean Core risk that teams don’t talk about enough?

Share your stories—this could help many teams avoid painful surprises.

entry.title: Re: Public Cloud DRC Turkey Input VAT Declaration
entry.content:
Dear Sebastian,

Report generation via the Run Statutory Reports app may fail without generating logs if the system is unable to retrieve records within the allocated runtime. In such cases, a runtime error occurs, and no application logs are created for the failed process.

To avoid this issue, it is recommended to execute report generation for longer reporting periods or high-volume data using the "Start a Long Run Now" option.

Kindly refer the below help document for your reference,

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/4de200b26d5d42bbacae69c6bf6b9923/542c7352dc194874b673f71281f2a938.html?locale=en-US&version=LATEST

If the issue persists and detailed error logs are still unavailable, it may be necessary to consult SAP support for further assistance.

 

Best regards,

Atanu

entry.title: Re: 10 reasons to trust the SAP Business Transformation Center with your data migration
entry.content:
@Sascha_Loeffler 

1. The Strategy Question

"With SAP BTC making Selective Data Transition (SDT) more accessible, are you seeing more of your clients move away from the 'Big Bang' Greenfield approach in favor of a more surgical, data-driven migration?"

2. The Clean Core/Footprint Question

"One of the biggest hurdles in S/4HANA migrations is deciding what history to leave behind. How are you leveraging BTC’s data footprint reduction tools to convince business stakeholders that 'less is more' when aiming for a Clean Core?"

3. The Security & Cloud Question

"Data residency is a major talking point for my U.S. Utility clients. SAP BTC keeps the actual migration traffic between the source and target without sensitive data hitting the cloud—has this security architecture changed the conversation for your more 'cloud-hesitant' stakeholders?"

4. The "Skills Gap" Question

"We often talk about the shortage of niche migration experts. Do you feel that the intelligent guidance and Fiori-based UX in SAP BTC will finally allow functional consultants to take a more active lead in data transformation, or will it remain a purely technical Basis/Developer task?"

5. The Real-World "War Story" Question

"In my experience, 'Pre-built content' is only as good as the edge cases it covers. For those who have already looked under the hood of SAP BTC, how has the predefined industry content handled your more complex, highly-customized legacy tables?"

entry.title: MvT 561 Reversal with Partial Consumption and Closed FI Period
entry.content:
Hello SAP Experts,

 

On 30.01.2026, I accidentally posted the document twice using MvT 561. The intended posting was MvT 712, but the option “Do Not Post Data” was not selected in ERP during the EWM process.

 

  • EWM transaction used: /SCWM/ISU
  • As a result, the stock was posted twice to ERP with MvT 561
  • Currently, part of the quantity has already been consumed in ERP (checked via MB52)
  • Additionally, the financial period is closed

 

 

Now, I would like to reverse the initial MvT 561 posting, but given the partial consumption and the closed FI period, I am unsure about the correct approach.

 

Could you please advise on possible standard SAP solutions or best practices to perform this reversal?

 

Thank you in advance for your support.

entry.title: Re: JMS Mastery Series Part 1 - The Async Awakening
entry.content:
The goal is to challenge the unexamined default toward synchronous patterns.

I like it because a great deal of time the default is to simply continue without much thought and falling into the category of "That's the way we've always done it."  You've provided a solid decision framework that helps you pare out the options quite easily.

To be honest I hadn't put much direct thought into the comparison between the two, but looking back since I have been using CPI instead of PI/PO I've quietly been developing all interfaces to be asynchronous and it was achieved using JMS.  I've shown prototypes for EM, but there hasn't been any appetite for it thus far.

I've experienced some very serious production down situations on account of trying to manage too much traffic in a synchronous manner (the cascading of blocking calls at volume), which is only exacerbated when someone has made the decision to increase the timeout without telling anyone.

This is an excellent topic and deserves much attention for anyone working in our space.

 

Regards,

Ryan Crosby

    • Does “Open in App” require any specific tenant or shell configuration?

entry.title: Restriction of Billing Document Cancellation Access
entry.content:
Dear Team,

We have provided a user with access to the Manage Billing Document application. However, we do not want to allow this user to cancel billing documents.

The requirement is that the user should only be able to post billing documents, not cancel them. Currently, both posting and cancellation are handled through the same application, which makes it difficult to restrict cancellation access.

Could you please suggest how we can achieve this access control, so that the user can post billing documents without having authorization to cancel them?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: Restriction of Billing Document Cancellation Access
entry.content:
Dear team, hope you're well!

Based on your description, I understand that you have a requirement to restrict or disable the use of "Cancel Billing Document" function in Manage Billing Documents app. 

As a standard system behavior it is not currently possible to disable or restrict the use of "Cancel Billing Document" function Manage Billing Documents app. 

However, as a workaround, there is an option to set the Billing type restrictions. For instance, it is possible to delete billing type S1 (or any other type of document) for "Write..." in the "Maintain Business Roles" app. 

Please see: How to Define Authorizations Based on Restrictions | SAP Help Portal

Note that this won't remove or disable the menu entry Cancel Billing Document or buttons, but it will prevent the user from creating cancellation documents.

See also: Business Roles, Business Catalogs, App Authorization Variants, Restrictions | SAP Help Portal

Addionally, you can also consider raising a feature request for this topic. Please see: 2963059 - How to Submit Feature Requests for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

Wish you a great day! 

Kind regards, 

Victoria

entry.title: The 2025 Developer Insights Survey: The Report
entry.content:
Role Overlaps for external SAP developers

Part I: Introduction and General Information​

Welcome to the 2025 SAP Developer Insights Survey report. This annual survey targets SAP’s existing external developer audience. It is used to track demographics, usage patterns, and trends within that population. 2025 is the fifth year we have conducted the survey. The survey program is led by the SAP Ecosystem and Community team. Key support is provided by the SAP Global Experience Management Office (XMO). Several other SAP teams provide advice and inputs to survey content. Those teams are listed at the end of this report.

The 2025 survey was open for six weeks - from January 22nd through March 7th.

Using social media announcements, SAP's Developer News show, and a web-popup on Community web sites, we invited all technical professionals in the SAP ecosystem to participate in the survey.

Each year's survey is composed of a mix of new topic questions and repeat select questions from past years to track trends.

Key Findings from the 2025 Survey​

  • Generative AI is Positively Affecting our Developer Ecosystem - While these tools are not exclusively Joule-based, roughly half of our respondents report evaluating Gen AI developer tooling and, among those using it, over half report that these tools have a "profound positive impact" in their real world use.

  • Low Code Use is Increasing - SAP Build low code tools lead in this space. Overall use of Low Code products on production projects continues to increase.

  • BTP Guidance Framework - limited awareness -- this important guidance document was announced at TechEd this past November, but - as with BTP Developers Guide in 2024 - barely half of respondents were aware of this document's existence.

  • Work-from-Home Continues Trending Down - we have been tracking these numbers since 2021. 6% fewer folks used their home as their primary workplace when compared to last year.

These topics will be covered in more detail in the rest of the report.

General Topics​

We included a number of general questions in the survey covering such topics as age, work location and status, as well as role.

Employment Status and Relation to SAP​

Employment

"What is your current employment status?"  
SAP Developer Community Composition
We have seen no significant change in these breakdowns from year to year.

SAP Developer Community Age Distribution

This shows increases in both the oldest age buckets for 2025. This was accompanied by similar decreases in the middle two age buckets ( ranging from 25 - 44 years of age). This merits deeper research as it potentially indicates either a gap in hiring younger employees to cover the normal aging of our community, or an outright loss of qualified younger staff. The two year trend in the 25-34 group reinforces that idea.

Where are the Developers?​

We asked, "Which country are you based in?"

By country, India has the largest number of respondents and probably the densest geographic concentration, followed by Germany, and then the USA. When grouped by SAP region, however, EMEA North is largest.

The proportions remain essentially unchanged from 2024.

Part II. Narrowing the Response Set

A primary objective of our survey is to build a model of our community's usage patterns. It helps us build more effective programs. With that goal in mind, in the remainder of the report we will narrow the data we'll consider in these ways:

  • External Developers Only - we exclude responses from SAP employees, and others (students, analysts, etc.)
  • Recent Development Experience - we only include responses from those who stated that they had been part of an SAP extension or integration project within the past year

When we filter the data set in this way, we are left with a set of 628 responses that we'll work with for the remainder of the report.

Workplace and Trends

Office Location
"As of right now, where do you primarily physically work from?"  
Year-to-year trend in Workplace
This shows a slowing trend of employees moving back to conventional offices from home. From a past Developer Survey, 29% of respondents reported they had primarily worked at home prior to the pandemic.

Recent SAP Project Experience?​

"Have you developed integrations or extensions for any SAP products or technologies in the past 12 months?" 

Extension and Integration are key scenarios for developers working with SAP applications. We wanted to focus on developers with recent experience in these areas. Roughly 70% of developers answered "Yes" here.

Popular Programming Languages​

"Which programming languages have you used in your development work over the past 12 months? Please select all that apply." 

This was posed as a multiple response question. ABAP dominates, as it has in every year of our survey. JavaScript is very popular as well, which could be attributed to its use in both SAPUI5 and CAP. Java and Python are roughly tied for third place. The relative positions of these top four languages hasn't changed much, year to year.

We extended our survey beyond just language use to ask about popular frameworks.

Popular Programming Frameworks​

We asked respondents who are Developers to pick which common front and back-end frameworks they have worked with in the past year.

Front-Ends

Back-Ends

Application and Business Technology Platform Architectures​

SAP applications can be extended using any one of several architectural approaches. These can be grouped into two major classes: on-stack (in-app) and side-by-side (essentially, BTP-based).

We were wondering which approaches are most popular.

Popular Extension Architectures

"For the most recent extension project that you were part of, what was the principal runtime architecture of the back-end elements?"  

We don't distinguish between newer and older SAP applications (e.g., S/4HANA vs. ECC) -- this likely explains the dominance of "on-stack ABAP". Aside from the obvious dominance of ABAP, we can also see that CAP is relatively popular. As with last year's results on this question, we see Low Code products like Build Apps and Build Process Automation are popular -- this question is asking about real production projects, and seeing those two account for roughly 10% of all extension projects highlights the value of these tools.

ABAP and CAP both have style or language variants in their respective frameworks. ABAP can be programmed as "ABAP for Cloud Development" or "ABAP Standard". CAP is available in JavaScript or Java language variants. Where the respondent selected ABAP or CAP, we asked about the variant used on the project:

 

ABAP Variants

CAP Variants

The rough 50-50 breakdown of ABAP styles is consistent with responses elsewhere in the survey. It reflects that roughly half of the ABAP projects are ECC or very early forms of S/4HANA. Importantly, the group that didn't understand the difference is considerably smaller than last year (2% versus 5% in 2024).

CAP/JavaScript is plainly most popular for CAP projects. Unlike last year, we do have respondents reporting the use of CAP/Java projects, although the percentage is modest - 5%.

Popular IDEs​

We are always curious about general work patterns of developers. We asked about developer's most commonly used editors. While it would be difficult in such a survey of all developers to add many questions, we were particularly curious about most popular IDE or editor.

 
Preferred IDE or Editor
"Which IDE or text editor do you most commonly use for development tasks?"  
Consistent with past year's results, the editor of choice will depend on the language. SAP GUI and -- arguably -- Eclipse are the primary editors for ABAP, so it's maybe no surprise that they are reflected as most popular. This is the first year that SAP Build Code appeared in the survey. We maintained separate choices for BAS and Build Code to see where folks might say that they have been using BAS, but have not yet transitioned to the broader capabilities offered by SAP Build Code.

Potential / Use of Generative AI for Developers​

Beginning with the 2024 survey, we asked developers about their thoughts and use of several major capabilities of Generative AI as it applies to software development.

Generative AI for Developers

"Which of these GenAI capabilities has your team explored or used? Select the choice that best describes your use of each capability. "  

An brief explanation of each category was included in the survey and is shown below:

Code Generation and Autocompletion: GenAI tools can generate code snippets, complete lines of code, or suggest entire functions based on the context provided by the developer.

Bug Detection and Code Review: AI models can be trained to identify potential bugs and vulnerabilities in the code. They can also suggest improvements in code quality, best practices, and code consistency.

Automated Testing and Test Data Generation: GenAI can be used to create test cases, which can help in thoroughly testing software applications. This includes generating test data that covers edge cases, potentially reducing the time and effort in manual testing.

Documentation Generation: AI can assist in generating and updating technical documentation. It can interpret the code and create comprehensive documentation, which is crucial for maintaining and scaling software projects.

Natural Language Processing (NLP) for Code: AI models can translate natural language queries into code, allowing developers to express their intent in plain language. This can be particularly helpful for novice developers working with unfamiliar frameworks.

Customized User Experience Design: AI can help generate user interface designs to enhance user experience.

AI-Powered Development Tools: Integration of AI in IDEs for real-time assistance, such as suggesting optimizations, refactoring code, or even predicting the next steps in development.

Automated Code Refactoring: AI can assist in code refactoring by suggesting or even implementing improvements in code structure.

Software Design and Architecture: AI can help in generating software design patterns or architectural models based on specified requirements. This can speed up the initial stages of development and ensure adherence to best practices.

Enhanced Security Protocols: Generative AI can be used to develop advanced security protocols and encryption methods, providing robust security solutions in software applications.

We also believed that Generative AI has been on the scene long enough for folks to being to experiment with putting it to use in meaningful ways. So, we included a couple of questions to gauge their perceptions of its utility to to their teams.

Current Gen AI Use

"How would you characterize your team’s current use of Generative AI tools or LLMs in your day-to-day work? Consider tools from any vendor or open source. Examples might include Joule, SAP Generative AI Hub, GitHub Copilot, or open source LLMs. (select the most applicable statement)"  

Gen AI Effectiveness for Developers

"Looking back on the past year, what is your own sense of how the use of Generative AI based tools or LLMs impacted the effectiveness or efficiency of your team?" 

Joule in SAP Build Code

Several Joule features were introduced into SAP Build Code prior to the survey. We asked respondents about their awareness or use of these features.

Joule for CDS Modeling

Joule for Test Data Set Generation

Joule Code Generation

Development Job Roles

We asked respondents which common development-related roles they perform.

"Which roles describe your day-to-day work? (Select all that apply)"

Respondents often "wear multiple hats" in their job. This question is asked in "select all that apply" form to capture that information.
The Venn diagram on the right depicts the relative overlaps of the top seven roles reported by respondents. The area of each overlapping region reflects the count respondents performing the overlapping roles. For example, from this we can seel that all (UX) "Designers" are also "Developers". Most but not quite all "Enterprise Architects" also are "Solution Architects".

These patterns of overlap are very similar to those in past years.

It is unsurprising that the "Developer" role leads. Architecture roles as a group have increased each year.

Development Domains​

For respondents selecting "Developer" as one of their roles, we were interested in the specific application Domains they work in:

"Which of the following development domains describe your day-to-day work? (select all that apply)"  
Year to year, Integration came in higher than we expected. Mobile web front-end development is more common than native mobile application development. This isn't completely unexpected, but worth noting.

 

Developer Satisfaction with SAP​

SAP Development Satisfaction
"For your recent experience developing an integration or extension, how would you describe your overall satisfaction with SAP?"  

Roughly 74% of respondents report they are “very satisfied” or “satisfied” with their development work with SAP. This is back near the 75% reported two years ago and also up from the 2024 figure, 70%.

We also gave all respondents a list of potential obstacles in their use of SAP tools and technologies and asked them to select the most significant pain points.

 
Obstacles in SAP tool us
"Which of these potential pain points are obstacles to your development when using SAP tools or frameworks? (select up to three most significant items)"  

BTP Guidance Framework

Awareness and Use of the BTP Guidance Framework
"The SAP BTP Guidance Framework was announced at SAP’s TechEd this past November. Which statement best described your level of familiarity with this document?"  
Nearly half of respondents were unaware of this document's release. Raising awareness for this and other related BTP framework documents would be a good goal for 2025.

Cloud Insights​

We first asked respondents if they used any cloud providers for their projects. For those that answered, "yes", we asked about the use of several major providers, including SAP BTP. We also asked if their use included SAP or non-SAP projects. This information was condensed into a chart depicting the relative use of each cloud provider.

Similar to the earlier questions around BTP Environments, these numbers do not reflect a tally of projects for each platform - instead it reflects developer exposure to each.

Using Cloud Providers?

"Are you using any cloud providers for your development projects?"  

We asked, "Which cloud providers are you using for development projects?" and we asked in a way to separate SAP-related projects from more general use.
 
SAP Business Technology Platform
Microsoft Azure

Amazon Web Services

 

Google Cloud Platform

Alibaba Cloud

Low / No Code Tools​

This is the third consecutive year in which we've asked questions around Low Code and No Code (LCNC) products. The list was composed of leaders in the then current Gartner LCNC Magic Quadrant Report and select SAP products. SAP Process Automation -- now Build Process Automation -- was not yet announced at the time of the 2022 survey, so it only appears in the more recent data.

For each product, we asked respondents to select the most applicable category reflecting their awareness or use. These questions were organized as a single large matrix of boxes in Qualtrics - There is no "I am unfamiliar with this product" response. Instead, the respondent simply would skip checking one of the other boxes.

It is important to point out that we are depicting response counts in the following charts rather than percentages and that the difference in total annual response counts must be considered to compare between years. The raw respondent count for 2023 was 2.4 times larger than 2022. And 2024 responses were less than half of 2023. We'll normalize for that in a later table.

These depictions can be misleading: our number of respondents each year varies. But we can normalize for that annual variation; what does this look like then? If we extract the data for production use for all products, we can use that to calculate the proportion use of each product by year (in other words, the sum of each column below totals one hundred percent). We can then visualize the relative shift in popularity over time:

 

Productive use of Product2022202320242025
Vendor A20%48%15%27%
SAP Build family (Build Apps + BPA)2%32%23%35%
Vendor B14%28%7%18%
SAP Signavio5%14%5%9%
Vendor C7%12%3%3%
Vendor D2%2%1%3%
Vendor E3%4%1%2%
Vendor F3%4%1%2%

SAP Build low code products dominate in our SAP ecosystem.

Learning and Help Resources​

We were interested in preferences in the format or media type of learning resources by respondents.

Preferred Learning Resources
"When learning new skills or technical subjects over the past 12 months, what resources did you tend use the most? Please select up to three resources."  

These results seem relatively unchanged year-to-year. One point of interest is the large difference in popularity between tutorials and MOOCs (53% versus 16% here). Tutorials tend to be hands-on. They are also broken into substantially smaller time chunks. This reinforces our hypothesis that different content types and required time investment have an impact in some learning scenarios. This merits investigation.

On the topic of Help, we shifted in this next question from formats to specific web sites.

 
Top SAP Help Resource Sites
"What is your preferred resource if you need help, or have a challenge with the SAP technology / tool you are working with? "  
Almost sixty percent of respondents say they use organic search or SAP Community as their primary source of SAP help. This is consistent with past years. This continues to demonstrate good visibility of the Community site overall and likely speaks well for the usefulness of the site content.

Satisfaction with SAP Learning Hub

SAP Learning Hub Awareness

"Which of the following best describes your exposure to the SAP Learning site?"

Overall Value of Learning Hub
"Overall how valuable are the resources on that site in helping you to understand SAP developer technologies?"  
Technical Certifications
"How many technology-related certification programs have you completed within the past four years?" 

Survey Methodology

This report is based on a Qualtrics web-based survey of 1,492 respondents from 58 countries. The survey ran for six calendar weeks between January 22 to March 7th, 2025. Ninety percent of the respondents invested ten minutes or less with the survey.

The survey was promoted via the SAP Community website and the SAP Developer Center, sap.com pop-up intercepts, social media posts.

Other References​

The annual Stack Overflow Developer Survey is an excellent (and no-cost) reference for industry-wide habits of developers.

entry.title: UI for the BTP Application Vulnerability Report
entry.content:
SAP recently introduced the Application Vulnerability Report for Cloud Foundry applications on BTP. It is a tool that scans your deployed applications for known vulnerabilities and exposes the findings through an API. If you haven't seen it yet, check out the official announcement: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/introducing-application-vulnerability-report-for-cloudfoundry-applications/ba-p/14281684

The API is great, but what was missing is an interface to browse, search, filter, sort and export those findings. So I built one. In this blog post I'll walk you through the open-source UI I created on top of the BTP Application Vulnerability Report API.

 

Problem

The Application Vulnerability Report API provides: vulnerability descriptions, severity levels, affected packages, CVE identifiers and the full organizational context (global account, sub-account, space, org). However, consuming a raw API to get an overview of your security posture is not practical for most teams. You need to be able to:

  • Quickly scan through all findings at a glance
  • Sort by severity, application name or date to prioritize remediation
  • Filter by sub-account, space, organization or any other column
  • Search across all findings with a free-text search
  • Export the full report to Excel for offline analysis or sharing with stakeholders

 

Solution

That's what I tried to solve by adding this UI: a full SAPUI5 frontend backed by a CAP Node.js service that proxies the BTP API, deployed via the managed approuter on BTP. The CAP layer might be a bit overkill at this point but it offers flexibility for future improvements.

The first version comes with the following features:

  • Sorting: Click column headers to sort findings
  • Filtering: Right-click cells or use column menus to filter by severity, application, sub-account, space or any field
  • Free-Text Search: Search across all finding properties using CVE numbers, package names or application names
  • Export to Excel: Generate formatted .xlsx files with all 15 fields including recommendations, dates, finding IDs and CVSS scores
  • Severity Indicators: Color-coded findings (Critical/High in red, Moderate in orange, Low in blue) for instant visual prioritization

 

Architecture Overview

The project consists of two parts:

  • CAP Node.js Backend: A lightweight OData V4 service that acts as a proxy to the BTP Application Vulnerability Report API. It connects to the external API via a BTP Destination with OAuth2 authentication. The CAP service defines the Findings entity as a projection on the external service model and uses api.run(req.query) to forward OData queries. At this point, the only value of having CAP is for merging some array fields into one single column:

 

 

  • SAPUI5 Frontend: A freestyle UI5 application with a table to show the overview and provide some additional functionalities like sorting, filtering and an export to excel. Now thinking about it, this could’ve been a Fiori Elements app as well. The app is deployed to the HTML5 Application Repository and served via the managed approuter of SAP Build Work Zone.

 

 

Getting It Up and Running

Prerequisites

  • Node.js >= 18
  • SAP CAP CLI (npm i -g @sap/cds-dk)
  • Cloud MTA Build Tool (npm i -g mbt)
  • CF CLI with the MTA plugin
  • A BTP subaccount with the Application Vulnerability Report service enabled

Step 1: Clone the Repository

git clone https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-application-vulnerability-report-ui.git
cd btp-application-vulnerability-report-ui
npm install

Step 2: Create the BTP Destination

In your BTP subaccount, create a destination named "BTPVulnerabilityReport" that points to the Application Vulnerability Report API. Configure it with OAuth2ClientCredentials authentication using the credentials from your service key.

Step 3: Build and Deploy

mbt build -t gen --mtar btpvulnerabilityreport.mtar
cf deploy gen/btpvulnerabilityreport.mtar

The MTA deployment creates:

  • A CAP Node.js backend application
  • An XSUAA service instance for authentication
  • A Destination service instance (with HTML5 runtime enabled)
  • An HTML5 Application Repository entry for the UI5 app

Step 4: Access the App

The application is deployed using the managed approuter pattern. You can access it through SAP Build Work Zone by adding it as an application tile. The Fiori Launchpad integration is pre-configured with the semantic object "VulnerabilityReport" and action "display".

Local Development

For local development, run cds watch from the project root (not ui5 serve from the app folder). The cds-plugin-ui5 plugin serves the UI5 app directly from the CAP server. To test against the real BTP API locally, use the hybrid profile: cds watch --profile hybrid after binding the destination service with cds bind.

No Database Required

The application does not use a database. All data comes directly from the BTP API in real-time. This keeps the architecture simple and ensures you always see the latest findings without any synchronization concerns.

 

What's Next?

The current version uses the managed approuter of SAP Build Work Zone for serving the application. I'm considering adding a standalone approuter configuration as an alternative for scenarios where Work Zone is not available or needed.

Other ideas for future enhancements include dashboard charts for severity distribution, drill-down to individual finding details and scheduled notifications for new critical findings.

Contributions and feedback are welcome! The full source code is available on GitHub:

https://github.com/lemaiwo/btp-application-vulnerability-report-ui

 

References

entry.title: Re: How do we trigger a Success Alert.
entry.content:
I was able to fix this by adding a simple groovy  script to trigger the success Alert, this keeps message status as successful and also sends me the alert for this. 

As in my requirement, if iflow starts, thats not enough, i have to have the process complete and file be written once that is done then i create and store a variable, if for some reason the sftp fails the variable does not get written and so i have to have wait for the variable to be written before i trigger the success alert, was relatively simple with groovy. 

Regards

Ravi

entry.title: Filter Block using Calculated Measure (RunningSum, Rank, etc.)
entry.content:
I'm trying to filter a block so that only branches making up the top 10% of sales for the whole company are listed.

In my report I have [Branch] and [Sales]. I have created variables for:

Total Sales
=Sum([Sales]) In Report

Sales Percent
=[Sales]/[Total Sales]

Rank
=Rank([Sales))

Cumulative Percent
=RunningSum([Sales Percent])

How can I make cumulative percent a static dimension that I can filter in the block? I've tried creating a flag variable, adding NoFilter/ForEach/In etc. to the other variables, and no matter what I do the block recalculates after the filter is added.

For error 6010 with MCP server in Joule Studio, if your SAP Build Process Automation(Joule Studio) and Joule are located in different sub account, please assure the same destination of MCP Servcer maintained in both subaccounts.
